skill.name: ai-video-generation
skill.description: Use the inference.sh belt CLI to generate, animate, edit, upscale, and add audio to AI videos with supported remote models.
skill.summary: Generate and edit AI videos through the inference.sh belt CLI.
skill.author: halt-catch-fire

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command and URL patterns, but review shows they are Markdown examples and documentation for the belt CLI rather than executable skill code. The remaining real risk is intentional: the skill authorizes belt commands and guides users to send prompts, image URLs, audio URLs, and video URLs to remote AI services.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Sends User Media Inputs to Remote AI Services
medium_findings.description: The skill grants Bash access for belt commands and provides examples that run remote video-generation apps. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but prompts and referenced media URLs can be transmitted to inference.sh-backed model providers.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and repeated belt app run examples clearly show intended external command execution. The commands are not malicious, but they create a real third-party data-sharing risk.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Hits Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The analyzer reported many Ruby or shell backtick execution hits. The reviewed lines are Markdown inline code, tables, and fenced examples for belt and npx commands, not embedded Ruby code or hidden executable scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged syntax appears in Markdown documentation and command examples. No evidence found of Ruby backtick execution or dynamic command construction.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Service Links and Placeholders
low_findings.description: The URL findings point to inference.sh documentation, a hosted image, install instructions, and placeholder media URLs. They do not show credential exfiltration or hidden callbacks, but users should avoid sending private media URLs without review.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation links or sample inputs. The privacy concern is contextual, not evidence of covert network behavior.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak cryptography hits do not correspond to hash, cipher, or certificate logic. The reviewed lines contain the skill description and a Markdown table header.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: No cryptographic operation, dependency, or algorithm is present at the cited locations. The static pattern appears to have matched unrelated text.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad belt CLI Command Permission
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill allows Bash execution for belt commands. This should be limited to trusted belt operations and reviewed before use with sensitive prompts or media.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools line is explicit and grants a command family. The risk depends on belt behavior and user-provided inputs.

content.user_title: Generate AI Videos with Remote Models
content.value_statement: Creating model-specific video commands is slow when each provider uses different inputs. This skill gives practical belt CLI examples for text-to-video, image animation, avatar video, editing, upscaling, and audio workflows.
actual_capabilities: Lists belt app IDs for text-to-video, image-to-video, avatar, lipsync, editing, upscaling, and foley workflows.
Provides copy-ready belt CLI examples for Veo, Seedance, HappyHorse, Wan, Grok, OmniHuman, Fabric, PixVerse, Topaz, and related tools.
Shows which input fields are used for prompts, image URLs, audio URLs, video URLs, duration, resolution, and generated audio.
Directs users to browse video apps through the belt app store category command.
Links related skills for broader inference.sh workflows, image generation, speech, and social publishing.
limitations: Requires the belt CLI and an inference.sh account before commands can run.
Generated video quality, speed, price, and availability depend on external model providers.
Examples rely on public or reachable media URLs for image, audio, and video inputs.
The skill does not include local rendering, local model weights, or built-in media editing code.

use_cases.title: Create social media video concepts
use_cases.description: Generate short clips from prompts, animate still images, and add sound effects for campaign drafts.

use_cases.title: Prototype product demo footage
use_cases.description: Test multiple video models and editing prompts before committing to a final creative direction.

use_cases.title: Build avatar and lipsync assets
use_cases.description: Turn portrait and speech inputs into talking-head clips for training, demos, or internal communication.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a simple video
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the AI video generation skill to create a short text-to-video command for a calm product reveal. Choose a fast model and explain the needed input fields.

prompt_templates.title: Animate an image
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a belt command to animate this image URL into a five-second video. Include a motion prompt and note any privacy concerns before running it.

prompt_templates.title: Compare model choices
prompt_templates.scenario: Selecting a model for a campaign
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare Veo, Seedance, HappyHorse, and Wan for a polished marketing clip. Recommend one model and provide a command template with placeholders.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a complete video pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a belt-based workflow that generates a clip, adds foley, upscales it, and merges it with another clip. Include each step and required inputs.

output_examples.input: A creator asks for a quick forest flyover video from a text prompt.
output_examples.output: The assistant selects a fast Veo model, prepares a belt command with the prompt, and explains that the request will run through inference.sh.

output_examples.input: A marketer provides a product image URL and wants subtle motion.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends an image-to-video model, adds a gentle camera motion prompt, and reminds the user to confirm the image URL can be shared.

output_examples.input: A trainer wants a portrait to speak from an audio file.
output_examples.output: The assistant chooses an avatar or lipsync model, identifies the image and audio inputs, and outlines the command sequence.

best_practices: Confirm that prompts and media URLs are safe to share with third-party services before running belt commands.
Start with fast or economical models for drafts, then rerun final prompts on higher-quality models.
Use clear duration, resolution, motion, and audio requirements so model inputs stay predictable.
anti_patterns: Do not send confidential customer media, unreleased product footage, or private portraits without approval.
Do not assume every listed model accepts the same input names, duration limits, or resolution values.
Do not run generated commands blindly when they include external URLs or account-specific settings.

faq.question: Does this skill generate videos locally?
faq.answer: No. It provides belt CLI guidance for remote inference.sh video apps.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Do I need an account?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill requires the belt CLI and a login before remote apps can run.

faq.question: Can it animate existing images?
faq.answer: Yes. The documentation includes image-to-video examples for several supported models.

faq.question: Can it create talking-head videos?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes avatar and lipsync examples that use image and audio inputs.

faq.question: What is the main security concern?
faq.answer: The main concern is sharing prompts and media URLs with external AI services through belt commands.
